name: Cyber Deconfliction
shortDescription: Coordinating cyber activity between authorised teams so defenders can tell sanctioned operations apart from real attacks — and so offensive teams don't trip over each other.
longDescription: Cyber deconfliction is the process of sharing — usually in advance — the *who, what, where, and when* of planned cyber activity so that other parties touching the same systems don't misinterpret it or interfere with it. Without deconfliction, a red-team engagement can trigger a full incident-response activation; two pen-test crews can scan each other and confuse evidence; a threat hunter can quietly burn a long-running surveillance operation by alerting the target. In practice, deconfliction takes the form of a shared channel (often a dedicated Slack/Teams room, ticket, or coordination cell) where the offensive side posts source IPs, target scope, time windows, and indicators they expect to generate, and the defensive side acknowledges so their SOC can suppress, tag, or quietly observe rather than escalate. The same mechanism is used between government agencies, between an MSSP and its customer, and between sister business units sharing infrastructure.
example: A purple-team exercise is scheduled to run from 02:00–06:00 UTC against the corporate VPN. Before kickoff, the red team posts to the #cyber-deconfliction channel: "Source: 198.51.100.42, target: vpn.corp.example.com, technique: password spraying against M365, expected IOCs: 4625 spikes from that IP, signed engagement letter ENG-2026-014." The SOC lead acknowledges and tags any matching alerts as `EXERCISE` so on-call analysts don't page the IR team at 03:00 and don't burn time pivoting on what looks like a real brute-force attack. After the window closes, the red team posts "complete" and the SOC re-arms the suppression rules. If, during the window, a *different* alert fires that doesn't match the deconfliction post, the SOC treats it as real — deconfliction reduces noise without disabling detection.

name: Per-Victim Server-Side Polymorphism
shortDescription: A delivery technique where the attacker's server generates a unique, freshly-mutated payload for every individual victim — so no two targets ever receive the same bytes, defeating hash- and signature-based detection.
longDescription: Per-victim server-side polymorphism shifts the classic polymorphic-malware idea (a payload that mutates itself between infections) onto the attacker's infrastructure. Instead of shipping a self-mutating binary and hoping the engine survives AV inspection, the operator runs a build/packing pipeline on their own staging server that re-generates the payload on demand: each HTTP request, phishing-link click, or loader callback triggers a fresh compile or repack with randomised junk code, reordered basic blocks, new encryption keys, swapped string obfuscation, different import resolutions, and a unique cryptographic signature. The victim downloads bytes that no other victim — and no AV vendor sample-sharing pipeline — has ever seen. Because mutation happens server-side, the operator can use heavy, slow, or non-portable tooling (LLVM passes, commercial protectors, custom obfuscators) that would be impractical to embed inside the malware itself. The technique cripples three common defences at once: file-hash blocklists become useless (every hash is unique); static YARA/AV signatures degrade sharply because surface features shift per build; and threat-intel sharing slows down because one organisation's IOC doesn't match another's. Defenders are pushed toward behavioural detection, EDR telemetry, network-side analysis of the *delivery* channel, and reputation/provenance signals (code signing, download source, parent process) rather than the payload bytes themselves. The trade-off for the attacker is operational: the mutation server becomes a high-value, easily-fingerprintable piece of infrastructure, and bugs in the build pipeline can produce broken samples that crash on the victim and burn the campaign.
example: An initial-access broker runs a malicious-document campaign. The phishing email contains a link to `https://cdn.example-delivery.tld/invoice/{victim_id}`. When a target clicks, the server looks up `victim_id`, kicks off a build job that (1) compiles a fresh loader stub from source with randomised function ordering and dead-code insertion, (2) encrypts the embedded second-stage shellcode with a freshly-generated AES key baked into the stub, (3) signs the result with one of a rotating pool of stolen code-signing certs, and (4) wraps everything in a one-shot Inno Setup installer with a unique installer GUID. The victim receives `Invoice_8421.exe` with SHA-256 `a91f…`; the next victim gets `Invoice_8422.exe` with SHA-256 `7d04…` — same behaviour, completely different bytes. When an EDR vendor finally captures one sample and publishes the hash 36 hours later, the blocklist protects exactly zero future victims, because no future victim will ever download those bytes. The defenders who *do* catch the campaign do so by spotting the loader's behaviour after execution (unusual parent process spawning `rundll32`, anomalous outbound TLS to a young domain) — not by matching the file.

name: Zip Smuggling
shortDescription: An evasion technique that abuses ambiguities and quirks in the ZIP archive format so different parsers (email gateway, AV scanner, end-user's archive tool) see different contents — letting a malicious file slip past inspection while still extracting cleanly for the victim.
longDescription: Zip smuggling exploits the fact that the ZIP format is parsed inconsistently across tools. A ZIP file has *two* parallel listings of its contents: a stream of local file headers at the front of the archive, and a central directory at the end. The format technically allows these to disagree, and different parsers prioritise them differently — some trust the central directory, some trust the local headers, some scan for either signature. Attackers weaponise this divergence to construct a single archive that looks benign to the inspecting tool and malicious to the extracting tool. Common variants include: (1) **concatenated ZIPs** — appending a second ZIP after the first, where some tools see only the first archive and others surface the second; (2) **mismatched central directory** — listing innocuous files in the central directory while the local headers point at a malicious payload (or vice versa); (3) **password-protected inner archives** — a benign outer ZIP containing an encrypted inner ZIP whose payload AV cannot scan, with the password supplied in the phishing email body or image; (4) **size/CRC mismatches** that cause some parsers to abort while others keep going; (5) **non-ASCII or overlong filenames** that one parser truncates and another treats as a different file type. The defender's gateway scans, sees `report.pdf` and `notes.txt`, marks the message clean. The user's WinRAR or 7-Zip walks the archive differently, surfaces `invoice.exe`, and the user double-clicks it. Defence requires parsing archives the same way the *endpoint* will (ideally with the same tool the user has), flagging archives whose two listings disagree, blocking nested/encrypted archives at the gateway, and preferring detonation in a sandbox over static parsing.
example: An attacker crafts `Q4_Report.zip` with a central directory listing two entries: `Q4_Report.pdf` and `cover_letter.docx`, both benign decoy files included verbatim. The local-file-header stream, however, contains a third entry — `Q4_Report.pdf .scr` (note the trailing space and `.scr` extension hidden by Windows' default file-name truncation in Explorer) — that is *not* referenced by the central directory. The corporate email gateway uses a ZIP library that walks the central directory, sees only the two clean files, scans them, and passes the message. The recipient saves the attachment and opens it in Windows Explorer's built-in ZIP viewer, which scans for local file headers instead and surfaces all three entries. The user, expecting a PDF, double-clicks the file that appears as `Q4_Report.pdf` (the trailing space and `.scr` are clipped from the visible name), and the screensaver-format executable runs the loader. Post-incident, the IR team confirms the same archive, when extracted with the gateway's library, yielded only two files; when extracted with the user's tool, yielded three — a textbook parser-divergence smuggling case.
